# Interprofessional practice, referral and looking after yourself

> *A safe clinician is defined as much by what they **refer** as by what they do. This chapter is about
> the edges of your role — advising well within them, handing over cleanly when a patient crosses them,
> working as a team, and looking after the one instrument you can't refer out: yourself.*

## Practice at a glance

Everything so far has been about *what* good dietary care looks like. This closing chapter of Part III
is about *where your role ends and someone else's begins*. It has three moves and a coda:

- **Advise** within scope — brief, general, evidence-aligned nutrition support any clinician can give.
- **Refer** when the need crosses into individualised therapy for a condition — that is a dietitian's work.
- **Work as a team** — nutrition is rarely a solo act, and Singapore has built a system that assumes it.
- **Mind yourself** — your own diet, sleep and activity are both a wellbeing issue and, plausibly, a
  credibility one.

The throughline is **humility with competence**: know your limits, refer well, work in a team, and
look after yourself.

## Knowing where your role ends

At this stage of training, your nutrition care is **brief, general, evidence-aligned and
patient-centred** — the *What a Healthy Diet Looks Like*, *Assessing Diet & Talking to Patients* and
*Behaviour-Change Counselling* chapters gave you exactly this toolkit. What it is *not* is individualised therapy for a
medical condition. Graduates consistently report feeling **under-prepared** to counsel on nutrition
[@crowley2019] — and the professional move is not to overreach to cover the gap, but to recognise it.
Scope is about **role and accountability, not just facts**: knowing the science is not a licence to
deliver treatment [@aclm2022comp; @afn2021curriculum]. Referral is a **competency and a safety
behaviour**, not an admission of failure.

## The line: advice versus therapy

There is a clean line between two kinds of "diet advice", and holding it is the core skill of this
chapter.

- **Universal advice (in scope).** My Healthy Plate, "cut the sugary drinks", "more vegetables, less
  refined rice", the dietary-pattern message — general guidance that is safe for almost anyone and that
  every clinician should give [@hpbHealthyPlate].
- **Medical nutrition therapy — MNT (refer).** An **individualised**, therapeutic nutrition plan for a
  medical condition, delivered by a **registered dietitian (RD)**: a structured cycle of assess →
  diagnose → intervene → monitor, evidence-based and outcome-tracked [@ada2026standards]. For type 2
  diabetes, RD-delivered MNT is associated with reductions in **HbA1c** (a marker of average blood-glucose control) of **roughly 0.3–2.0%** — a
  guideline-reported range across trials of differing intensity, not a guaranteed effect
  [@ada2026standards].

Conflating the two fails in both directions: treat all advice as MNT and you freeze, too afraid to say
anything safe; treat MNT as ordinary advice and you overstep into care you can't be accountable for.

Run a few real patients through the line below — the same question ("should I advise, or refer?") lands
very differently depending on complexity.

<div data-island="advise-refer" data-src="/diagrams/advise-refer.yml"></div>

## Referral triggers

You don't have to judge each case from scratch — a short checklist catches most of it. **Refer to a
dietitian when** there is:

- a **complex or multiple condition** — type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, cardiovascular
  disease, GI disease (the conditions you'll meet in Part IV, the disease chapters that follow);
- **unintended weight loss or malnutrition risk** (the screen covered later in *Undernutrition & Malnutrition*);
- **obesity needing structured management**, or **pregnancy / paediatric** nutrition;
- a **food allergy or intolerance**, or any **eating-disorder** concern;
- or simply that the advice needed **exceeds general guidance**, or the patient **isn't progressing**.

Notice these are *recognition* triggers, not treatment cues: your job is to spot them and hand over,
not to work up the plan yourself.

## Writing a referral that helps

A referral is only as useful as what you put in it, and a scarce resource is wasted by a vague one.
"Please advise on diet" tells the dietitian nothing. A referral that *helps* carries:

::: {.callout-note}
## What a good dietitian referral contains
- **Reason for referral** and its **urgency**.
- **Relevant diagnoses** (and how recent).
- **Current medications**.
- **Key results** — e.g. HbA1c, lipids, renal function (eGFR).
- **Weight / BMI and the recent trend** (gaining, stable, losing).
- **The patient's goals and social context** — who cooks, whether they eat out, whether they live alone.
:::

Completeness is itself a clinical skill: it turns a hand-off into a head start.

## Nutrition is a team sport

Nutrition rarely acts alone. Around any patient with a chronic condition sits a team, each with a
distinct hand-off: the **family doctor** coordinates; the **registered dietitian** delivers MNT; the
**nurse** monitors and educates; the **pharmacist** reviews medications; and **allied-health and
community partners** extend care beyond the clinic. Team-based care is associated with better
chronic-disease outcomes — the whole is more than any one professional's slice [@aclm2022comp;
@afn2021curriculum].

That "more than one slice" cuts both ways, and it is a common trap to over-claim the nutrition part.
The headline lifestyle results you may have met were **team-delivered and intensive**, not a clinic
chat: DiRECT's diabetes remission ran on a structured total-diet-replacement programme in **selected**
patients (early type 2 diabetes, not on insulin), and the DPP's prevention effect came from a
**16-session** curriculum [@lean2018direct; @dpp2002]. As the *Evidence vs Hype* chapter argued, don't
promise those outcomes from advice alone.

## The Singapore picture — Healthier SG

Singapore has built the scaffold that makes referral and follow-up actually happen. **Healthier SG**
(national roll-out from 2023) gives every resident **one enrolled family doctor** who co-develops a
personalised **Health Plan** — the natural place a nutrition concern is raised and referred
[@mohHealthierSG]. It is deliberately **team-based and community-linked**: family doctors can make
**social prescriptions** to community programmes (Health Promotion Board activities, Active Ageing
Centres, SportSG), and a **tiered dietitian pathway** has clinicians give first-line dietary advice and
**escalate complex cases to a registered dietitian** [@mohEatWiseSG] — your "advise vs refer" line,
built into the health system.

Funding follows the same logic. The **Healthier SG Chronic Tier** (from 2024) gives enrolled
CHAS / Pioneer Generation / Merdeka Generation cardholders **means-tested subsidies of up to about
87.5% on selected chronic medications** (with no dollar cap) plus **up to about \$360 a year** toward
other care components such as consultations and tests — the mechanism that keeps chronic-disease
follow-up affordable [@mohChronicTier]. One caveat to flag for students: these
materials centre on **medications and care components**, and do **not** spell out a dedicated
dietitian / MNT subsidy line — RD and allied-health access typically flows via polyclinic, specialist
and cluster services, and the exact funding evolves. Teach the **pathway and the principle**, and
check current figures before quoting them to a patient.

![Healthier SG puts an enrolled family doctor at the centre of a team-and-community model: a shared Health Plan, hand-offs to the dietitian, nurse and pharmacist, social prescribing to community partners, and Chronic Tier subsidies that keep follow-up affordable.](/figures/mechanisms/hsg-ecosystem.svg){#fig-hsg-ecosystem}

## Physician, mind thyself

The last part of your scope is the one clinicians skip: yourself. Clinicians who look after their own
diet and activity are observed to **counsel more often, and arguably more credibly** — the evidence is
**associational**, not proof of cause, but it is consistent [@lobelo2015rolemodel; @lianov2010]. And
your own habits are not just a role-model question. As the *Appetite & Weight Regulation* chapter set
out, **short sleep shifts the appetite hormones** — raising ghrelin, blunting satiety and pushing
intake up — so the packed-roster diet of skipped meals, *kopi* and five hours' sleep quietly works
against your own metabolic health [@sleepRestriction]. The reply is not an overhaul but a **sustainable
micro-plan**: one realistic food swap, a **sleep anchor**, a short daily walk. Practitioner wellbeing
is a professional and patient-safety issue, not an indulgence — and burnout is a **longitudinal skill**
to keep working at, not a box to tick once [@lianov2010].

## So — where does your role end?

Not at the edge of what you know, but at the edge of what you can safely be **accountable** for. You
**advise** within scope, you **recognise the triggers** and **refer** — writing a referral complete
enough to be a head start, not a shrug — you **work with the team** the system already gives you, and
you **look after yourself** so you can keep doing it. Knowing your limits is not a weakness in a
clinician; it is one of the clearest marks of a safe one. Carrying a whole encounter live, with a
simulated patient, is the work of the capstone chapter that follows.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/interprofessional-referral.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: refer or advise? Write the referral

Mdm Tan, 58, is enrolled with her Healthier SG family doctor. She has newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes
(HbA1c 9.1%), early kidney impairment (eGFR 52), takes metformin and two other agents, has a BMI of 29
(obese on Asian cut-offs), eats mostly hawker food and lives alone. Decide what is in your scope, what
crosses into a dietitian's, and — if you refer — what the referral must say. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/refer-or-advise.case.yml"></div>
